pattern_recognition: Dynamic Programming - This problem is categorized under dynamic programming as it involves breaking down the problem into smaller subproblems (counting unique BSTs for smaller values of n) and using previously computed results to build up the solution for larger values of n. The signals include the need to count combinations of structures based on recursive definitions.

approaches.title: Brute Force
approaches.intuition: Generate all possible BSTs for n nodes and count the unique structures.
approaches.algorithm: Use recursion to generate all possible trees by selecting each node as the root and recursively generating left and right subtrees.

approaches.title: Optimal Approach
approaches.intuition: Use dynamic programming to compute the number of unique BSTs using previously computed results for smaller values of n.
approaches.algorithm: Define an array dp where dp[i] represents the number of unique BSTs that can be formed with i nodes. Use the relation dp[i] = sum(dp[j-1] * dp[i-j]) for j from 1 to i.

dry_run: For n = 3, we can visualize the unique BSTs: 1 as root gives two structures (with 2 and 3 as right children), 2 as root gives one structure (1 as left child and 3 as right child), and 3 as root gives two structures (with 1 and 2 as left children). Thus, the total unique BSTs = 5.
edge_cases: n = 0 (should return 1 as there's one empty tree), n = 1 (should return 1 as there's one tree with a single node), large n (ensure performance and memory efficiency), and n = 2 (should return 2 as there are two unique BSTs).
common_pitfalls: 1) Forgetting to initialize the dp array correctly can lead to incorrect results. 2) Not considering all possible root nodes when generating trees can miss valid structures. 3) Misunderstanding the recursive relation can lead to incorrect counts of unique BSTs.
